content.problemStatement:
Given an array of integers, count all triplets where sum of any two elements equals the third element. A triplet can have any arrangement of elements (i.e., sum of first two equals third, or sum of first and third equals second, etc.).


Input Format

First line contains TTT, the number of test cases
For each test case:

First line contains NNN
Second line contains NNN space-separated integers




Output Format

For each test case, print the count of valid triplets.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the size of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers a1, a2, …, aN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the number of unordered triplets (i, j, k) with distinct indices such that the sum of any two of the three elements equals the third element.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: Triplets are (1,2,3) and (2,3,5).\nTest case 2: No two numbers sum to a third.\nTest case 3: Valid triplets are (1,2,3), (1,3,4), (1,4,5) and (2,3,5).
The only unordered pair of distinct indices is the two 1's; their sum is 2, which exists once, giving one triplet.
The pair of the two 2's sums to 4, and there are two distinct indices holding 4, so two different triplets are formed.

solution.approach: For each test case we store the frequency of every value (the maximum value is 100000, so a simple array works). Then we iterate over all unordered pairs of indices (i < j). Let s = a[i] + a[j]. If s is within the allowed range we add freq[s] to the answer. Because all ai are positive, s is strictly larger than a[i] and a[j]; therefore any index counted in freq[s] is automatically different from i and j, guaranteeing distinct indices. Each valid unordered triplet has exactly one ordered pair (the two smaller numbers) that sums to the largest one, so it is counted exactly once. The algorithm runs in O(N^2) time per test case and uses O(MAXV) extra memory for the frequency array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case (N ≤ 1000, so at most 10^6 operations).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(MAXV) = O(100000) for the frequency array.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    MAXV = 100000
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        arr = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        freq = [0] * (MAXV + 1)
        for v in arr:
            freq[v] += 1
        ans = 0
        for i in range(N):
            ai = arr[i]
            for j in range(i + 1, N):
                s = ai + arr[j]
                if s <= MAXV:
                    ans += freq[s]
        out.append(str(ans))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
